title: Lecture on Angelini et al. (2020)
text:
4 What are we seeing here?
\[
y =
\mu
+ \text{Replicate}
+ \text{Year}
+ \text{SowingTime}
+ \text{WholePlotError}
+ \text{Cultivar}
+ \text{Interactions}
+ \text{SubplotError}
\]

Conceptual model:
\[
y_{ij} = \mu + B_i + T_j + \epsilon_{ij}
\]
Where:

\(y_{ij}\) = observed response in block \(i\) receiving treatment \(j\)
\(\mu\) = overall mean response
\(B_i\) = effect of block \(i\)

Accounts for background variation we are not primarily interested in
Examples: lake, field, site, year
Typically treated as a random effect

\(T_j\) = effect of treatment \(j\)

The main effect we want to study
Treated as a fixed effect

\(\epsilon_{ij}\) = residual error

Random unexplained variation
Represents variability among experimental units within blocks
Assumed: \[
\epsilon_{ij} \sim \text{Normal}(0, \sigma^2)
\]


Key idea:
There is only ONE level of experimental units and ONE source of experimental error.

title: Lecture on Angelini et al. (2020)
text:
5 Two Error Strata



Stratum
Tests
Unit




Whole-plot error
Sowing Time
Whole plot in block


Subplot error
Genotype, G×ST
Subplot in whole plot









Important



Using a single pooled error gives an anti-conservative test for ST and an over-conservative test for G. This is one of the most common errors in agronomy papers.

title: Lecture on Angelini et al. (2020)
text:
6 aov() vs. lmer() for Split-Plots
Classical approach — explicit error strata:
aov(yield ~ ST * G * Year + Error(Block/ST), data = dat)
What lmerTest does:
lmer(yield ~ G * ST * Year + (1|Block) + (1|Block:ST), data = dat)
anova(model)  # Satterthwaite df

Both are valid. But lmer output labeled as “ANOVA” obscures that this is a linear mixed model with approximated denominator df.

Welcome everyone. Here is the road map for today. We have a 75-minute class. The first half belongs to our presenter — they will walk us through Gleim et al. (2014), the tick phenology and prescribed burning paper. The second half belongs to methods — we’ll dig into GEE (Generalized Estimating Equations), which is the statistical engine the authors used. Before the presentation starts, I need everyone to fill out a quick entry ticket — paper out or open the CSV on your laptop. Five minutes, not graded.

title: Tick Phenology & Prescribed Burning: GEE for Repeated-Measures Count Data
text:
2 Entry Ticket Debrief






What I saw in your entry tickets




Research objective: Long-term prescribed burning effects on tick population dynamics
Outcome variable: Tick counts per plot per month (count, overdispersed)
Clusters: 21 sampling plots (not individual ticks!)
Time structure: Monthly repeated measures over 2 years (24 time points)
Method: GEE with negative binomial family, log link, exchangeable correlation



. . .
Common question from entry tickets: “Why not just use a mixed model?” — We’ll come back to this.

I want to highlight a few patterns I saw. A lot of you correctly identified the outcome as tick counts — great. Several of you said the clusters were “observations” or “individual ticks” — that’s the most important misconception to address today. The cluster is the PLOT. There are 21 plots; each plot was measured monthly for 2 years. The 24 time points within each plot are the repeated measures. Also, many of you asked “why not a mixed model?” — excellent question, and we will spend time on exactly that distinction today.

title: Tick Phenology & Prescribed Burning: GEE for Repeated-Measures Count Data
section: 3 Why GEE? Clustered Data & Marginal Inference
text:
3 Why GEE? Clustered Data & Marginal Inference
The data structure problem:

21 plots, each measured 24 times (monthly, 2010–2011)
Tick counts within a plot are correlated (not independent)
Ordinary GLM assumes independence → inflated Type I error

. . .
Two solutions:








Approach
What it models
Interpretation




GLMM
Conditional mean \(E[Y \mid \mathbf{b}]\)
Effect within a specific plot


GEE
Marginal mean \(E[Y]\)
Effect averaged across all plots



. . .

For policy-relevant questions — “Does burning reduce ticks on average?” — GEE is the natural choice.


This is the conceptual heart of the lecture. When you have repeated measures or clustered data, you have two philosophically different questions you can ask. GLMM asks: “for THIS plot, what is the effect of burning?” GEE asks: “averaged across ALL plots, what is the effect of burning?” For a public health or wildlife management question like “should we prescribe burn to reduce tick exposure across a landscape?”, the marginal, population-averaged answer from GEE is exactly what we want. Conditional effects from GLMM are appropriate when we care about prediction for a specific cluster.

title: Tick Phenology & Prescribed Burning: GEE for Repeated-Measures Count Data
text:
4 The GEE Estimating Equation
\[
\sum_{i=1}^{K} \mathbf{D}_i^\top \mathbf{V}_i^{-1}\left(\mathbf{Y}_i - \boldsymbol{\mu}_i\right) = \mathbf{0}
\]







Symbol
Meaning




\(K = 21\)
Number of plots (clusters)


\(\mathbf{Y}_i\)
Observed tick counts for plot \(i\) (length 24)


\(\boldsymbol{\mu}_i = E[\mathbf{Y}_i \mid \mathbf{X}_i]\)
Predicted marginal means


\(\mathbf{D}_i = \partial \boldsymbol{\mu}_i / \partial \boldsymbol{\beta}\)
Derivative of mean w.r.t. parameters


\(\mathbf{V}_i = \phi\,\mathbf{A}_i^{1/2} R(\alpha) \mathbf{A}_i^{1/2}\)
Working covariance


\(R(\alpha)\)
Working correlation matrix




Let’s slow down here. This equation looks intimidating but the logic is simple. GEE is a score equation — we’re finding the beta that sets the weighted sum of residuals to zero. The key quantity is V_i — the working covariance matrix. This is where we specify our assumption about how the 24 monthly tick counts within a plot are correlated. A_i is a diagonal matrix of variances (from the negative binomial family). R(alpha) is the working correlation — exchangeable, AR(1), or independence. And here is GEE’s great advantage: even if R(alpha) is wrong, the estimates are still consistent. We don’t have to get the correlation right to get correct point estimates.

title: Tick Phenology & Prescribed Burning: GEE for Repeated-Measures Count Data
text:
5 Sandwich (Robust) Variance
\[
\widehat{\text{Var}}(\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}) = \mathbf{B}^{-1} \mathbf{M} \mathbf{B}^{-1}
\]

\(\mathbf{B} = \displaystyle\sum_i \mathbf{D}_i^\top \mathbf{V}_i^{-1} \mathbf{D}_i\)   (the bread — model-based information)
\(\mathbf{M} = \displaystyle\sum_i \mathbf{D}_i^\top \mathbf{V}_i^{-1} \widehat{\text{Cov}}(\mathbf{Y}_i) \mathbf{V}_i^{-1} \mathbf{D}_i\)   (the meat — empirical)

. . .

Key insight: The sandwich variance is consistent even when \(R(\alpha)\) is misspecified, provided \(K\) (clusters) is large enough.







Warning



With only \(K = 21\) clusters, the sandwich estimator may be anti-conservative (too narrow CIs). Small-sample corrections (e.g., std.err = "jack" in geepack) are advisable.



The sandwich estimator gets its name from the formula: B-inverse times M times B-inverse. Bread times meat times bread. B is the expected curvature of the likelihood — it’s what you’d use if your working covariance was exactly right. M is the empirical estimate of the actual variance — it uses the observed residuals, not the model-assumed residuals. When you sandwich M between two B-inverses, you get a variance estimate that is robust to correlation misspecification. HOWEVER — and this is critical for this paper — with only 21 clusters, the sandwich estimator can be unreliable. The standard recommendation is at least 40 clusters for stable sandwich SEs. The authors of the tick paper had 21 plots; this is a legitimate limitation worth mentioning.

title: Tick Phenology & Prescribed Burning: GEE for Repeated-Measures Count Data
text:
6 The Paper’s Model Specification






Note



Note: Gleim et al. (2014) do not provide an explicit model equation. The following is an inferred defensible specification based on their Methods section (p. 3).


\[
\log\!\bigl(E[\text{ticks}_{it}]\bigr) = \beta_0 + \beta_1\,\text{BurnRegime}_i + \beta_2\,\text{HostAbundance}_{it} + \beta_3\,\text{Canopy}_{it} + \beta_4\,\text{Season}_{it} + \cdots
\]

Family: Negative binomial (overdispersed counts; 47,185 total; 99% A. americanum)
Link: Log (counts → multiplicative effects on tick abundance)
Correlation: Exchangeable (equal correlation among all 24 monthly observations per plot)
Clusters: 21 plots (7.2–78.9 ha); ≥10 years of consistent burn management
Predictors: Burn regime (BB, BUB, UBB, UBUB), host abundance (cameras), canopy closure, tree density, ground cover, temperature, precipitation


Here is the model as best we can infer it. The paper describes using GEE with negative binomial family and log link in a sentence or two, but never displays a formal equation or coefficient table in the main text. This is unfortunately common in applied ecology. The negative binomial family is appropriate because tick counts are highly overdispersed — the variance far exceeds the mean. The log link gives us multiplicative effects — a one-unit change in burn regime multiplies the expected tick count by e to the beta. Exchangeable correlation means the model assumes the correlation between plot 7’s tick counts in January and December is the same as between January and February. That may not be realistic — seasonal data often shows decaying autocorrelation better captured by AR(1).

title: Tick Phenology & Prescribed Burning: GEE for Repeated-Measures Count Data
text:
7 Working Correlation Structures









Structure
Formula
Assumption
Best for…




Independence
\(R = I\)
Zero correlation
Sensitivity check; large \(K\)


Exchangeable
\(\text{Corr}(Y_{it}, Y_{is}) = \alpha\)
Equal correlation at all lags
Short series; random effects analogy


AR(1)
\(\text{Corr}(Y_{it}, Y_{is}) = \alpha^{|t-s|}\)
Correlation decays with lag
Time series; seasonal ecological data



. . .

For monthly tick counts over 2 years, AR(1) is more ecologically realistic than exchangeable — but GEE estimates are consistent under either.


Model selection: Use QIC (quasi-likelihood under independence model criterion) to compare working correlation structures.


This table is worth spending a couple of minutes on because it connects directly to the group worksheet. Independence assumes no correlation — use this as a baseline or sensitivity check. Exchangeable assumes that correlation is constant regardless of the time gap — that the correlation between observations 1 month apart is the same as observations 23 months apart. That’s a strong assumption for ecological time series. AR(1) assumes correlation decays exponentially with lag — more realistic for monthly data where nearby months are more similar. For this study, AR(1) seems more defensible, but the authors chose exchangeable. This is exactly the kind of analytic choice worth critiquing.

Answer as many of the 6 critique prompts as you can
Your main deliverable: One “challenge question” for the presenter

Critique prompts: 1. Is negative binomial appropriate? Alternatives? 2. Is exchangeable correlation realistic for monthly ecological data? 3. Are cluster sizes balanced? How might imbalance affect GEE? 4. The paper doesn’t report the full model equation — is this a transparency issue? 5. How does the sandwich variance protect against correlation misspecification? 6. What would change if they used GLMM instead of GEE?



Circulate among groups. Push back if groups are writing softball questions. Good challenge questions for the presenter are things like: “Can you justify why exchangeable correlation is more appropriate than AR(1) for monthly sampling?” or “The paper has 21 clusters — is the sandwich estimator reliable?” These are questions that require genuine methodological reasoning, not just paper summary.

title: 1 Expanded Lecture Notes: GEE for Repeated-Measures Count Data
text:
These notes expand on the slide deck (slides/slides-ticks-gee.qmd) and provide: - Short derivation of the GEE estimating equation from quasi-likelihood - Sandwich variance derivation (bread and meat) - Working correlation comparison (pros, cons, guidance) - Why negative binomial over Poisson for overdispersed counts - Connection to the specific design and analysis in Gleim et al. (2014) - Key references




Introduce the paper briefly: it is an applied ecology paper asking whether long-term prescribed burning reduces tick abundance. The paper is notable for using GEE to account for repeated sampling within plots — a correct and defensible choice, though the methods reporting could be more transparent.




Orient students to the structure of the 75-minute class. Emphasize that the student presenter leads the first half; the second half is instructor-led methods and group activity. The minute paper at the end feeds into next class.




Fill in the bullet points based on what you actually saw in entry tickets. Key corrections to make: - Cluster = plot (not individual ticks) - Outcome = count data → appropriate distribution choices are Poisson or negative binomial - GEE handles the within-plot repeated measures structure






The distinction between marginal and conditional models is foundational.
Conditional model (GLMM): \[
\log(E[Y_{it} \mid b_i]) = \mathbf{X}_{it}^\top \boldsymbol{\beta} + b_i
\] where \(b_i \sim N(0, \sigma^2)\) is a random effect for plot \(i\).
The interpretation of \(\boldsymbol{\beta}\) here is: the effect of a one-unit change in a covariate on the log-expected tick count for a plot with random effect \(b_i\). The coefficient is plot-specific.
Marginal model (GEE): \[
\log(E[Y_{it}]) = \mathbf{X}_{it}^\top \boldsymbol{\beta}
\]
Here \(\boldsymbol{\beta}\) is the population-averaged effect — the average effect across all plots.



For the tick burning question: “Does prescribed burning reduce tick abundance on average across the landscape?” — the marginal GEE answer is directly what we want. If we asked “what is the tick reduction in plot 7 specifically?”, GLMM would be more appropriate.
In large-cohort observational studies (where GEE originated in biostatistics), the population-averaged effect is almost always the policy-relevant quantity.







Let: - \(K\) = number of clusters (21 plots) - \(n_i\) = number of observations in cluster \(i\) (up to 24 months) - \(Y_{it}\) = tick count for plot \(i\) at month \(t\) - \(\mu_{it} = E[Y_{it} \mid \mathbf{X}_{it}]\) = marginal mean - \(\mathbf{Y}_i = (Y_{i1}, \ldots, Y_{in_i})^\top\), \(\boldsymbol{\mu}_i = (\mu_{i1}, \ldots, \mu_{in_i})^\top\)



For a generalized linear model (GLM) with response vector \(\mathbf{Y}_i\) and mean \(\boldsymbol{\mu}_i\), the quasi-likelihood score equation for a single cluster would be:
\[
\mathbf{S}_i(\boldsymbol{\beta}) = \mathbf{D}_i^\top \mathbf{V}_i^{-1} (\mathbf{Y}_i - \boldsymbol{\mu}_i)
\]
where: - \(\mathbf{D}_i = \frac{\partial \boldsymbol{\mu}_i}{\partial \boldsymbol{\beta}}\) is the \(n_i \times p\) Jacobian of the mean w.r.t. parameters - \(\mathbf{V}_i\) is the working covariance matrix of \(\mathbf{Y}_i\)
Summing over all clusters and setting equal to zero gives the GEE:
\[
\sum_{i=1}^{K} \mathbf{D}_i^\top \mathbf{V}_i^{-1} (\mathbf{Y}_i - \boldsymbol{\mu}_i) = \mathbf{0}
\]



\[
\mathbf{V}_i = \phi \, \mathbf{A}_i^{1/2} \, R(\alpha) \, \mathbf{A}_i^{1/2}
\]
where: - \(\phi\) = dispersion parameter (estimated from data; for negative binomial, this is the overdispersion) - \(\mathbf{A}_i\) = diagonal matrix with \(\text{Var}(\mu_{it})\) on the diagonal (depends on the family; for Poisson: \(\mu_{it}\); for negative binomial: \(\mu_{it} + \mu_{it}^2/\theta\)) - \(R(\alpha)\) = working correlation matrix



Liang & Zeger (1986) showed that if the mean structure \(\boldsymbol{\mu}_i = h(\mathbf{X}_i \boldsymbol{\beta})\) is correctly specified, then \(\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}\) is consistent regardless of whether \(R(\alpha)\) is the true correlation matrix. This is GEE’s defining advantage.







The GEE estimator \(\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}\) solves \(\sum_i \mathbf{S}_i(\boldsymbol{\beta}) = \mathbf{0}\).
By a Taylor expansion:
\[
\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}} - \boldsymbol{\beta}_0 \approx \left[\sum_i \frac{\partial \mathbf{S}_i}{\partial \boldsymbol{\beta}}\right]^{-1} \sum_i \mathbf{S}_i(\boldsymbol{\beta}_0)
\]
The asymptotic variance of \(\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}\) is:
\[
\text{Var}(\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}) = \mathbf{B}^{-1} \, \text{Var}\!\left(\sum_i \mathbf{S}_i\right) \mathbf{B}^{-1}
\]
where \(\mathbf{B} = \sum_i \mathbf{D}_i^\top \mathbf{V}_i^{-1} \mathbf{D}_i\) (the bread).
Since the clusters are independent, \(\text{Var}(\sum_i \mathbf{S}_i) = \sum_i \text{Var}(\mathbf{S}_i)\).
Estimating \(\text{Var}(\mathbf{S}_i)\) empirically gives the meat:
\[
\mathbf{M} = \sum_i \mathbf{D}_i^\top \mathbf{V}_i^{-1} \widehat{\text{Cov}}(\mathbf{Y}_i) \mathbf{V}_i^{-1} \mathbf{D}_i
\]
where \(\widehat{\text{Cov}}(\mathbf{Y}_i) = (\mathbf{Y}_i - \hat{\boldsymbol{\mu}}_i)(\mathbf{Y}_i - \hat{\boldsymbol{\mu}}_i)^\top\).
The sandwich estimator is:
\[
\widehat{\text{Var}}(\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}) = \mathbf{B}^{-1} \mathbf{M} \mathbf{B}^{-1}
\]



The sandwich estimator assumes \(K \to \infty\). With \(K = 21\) clusters (as in this paper), it can be anti-conservative. Corrections include: - Jackknife SE: std.err = "jack" in geepack::geeglm - Pan (2001) small-sample correction - Bootstrap SEs (resample entire clusters)







Gleim et al. (2014) state (paraphrasing from Methods, p. 3):

“We used generalized estimating equations (GEE) with a negative binomial distribution and log link function to account for the repeated monthly sampling within plots. The exchangeable working correlation structure was used.”

The paper does not display a formal model equation, parameter table, or explicit predictor list in a single model specification table. Predictors mentioned in the text include: burn treatment (BB, BUB, UBB, UBUB), host abundance indices (trail camera data), vegetation structure (canopy closure, tree density, ground cover), and climatic variables.



A defensible inferred specification is:
\[
\log(E[\text{ticks}_{it}]) = \beta_0 + \beta_1 \cdot \text{BurnRegime}_i + \beta_2 \cdot \text{HostAbundance}_{it} + \beta_3 \cdot \text{Canopy}_{it} + \beta_4 \cdot \text{Season}_{it} + \cdots
\]
Subscript \(i\) = plot (cluster), \(t\) = month (time point within cluster).



For a log-link model: - \(\exp(\hat{\beta}_1)\) = multiplicative change in expected tick count per unit change in \(X_1\) - If \(\hat{\beta}_1 = -0.8\) for UBB vs UBUB: \(\exp(-0.8) \approx 0.45\) → 55% reduction in tick counts in UBB plots compared to UBUB plots







\[
R(\alpha) = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & \alpha & \alpha & \cdots \\ \alpha & 1 & \alpha & \cdots \\ \vdots & & \ddots & \\ \alpha & \cdots & \alpha & 1 \end{pmatrix}
\]
Pros: Simple; one parameter to estimate; analogous to random intercept model; well-behaved with small \(K\).
Cons: Assumes January–February correlation = January–December correlation. Unrealistic for time series.



\[
R_{ts} = \alpha^{|t-s|}
\]
Pros: Ecologically realistic for monthly data (closer months more correlated); one parameter.
Cons: Requires equally spaced time points; can behave oddly with missing data.



\[
R = I
\]
Pros: Simplest; robust with large \(K\); equivalent to ignoring clustering (but using sandwich SEs).
Cons: Least efficient (widest CIs if true correlation is non-zero).



The QIC (Pan 2001) is used to compare GEE models. It is analogous to AIC but accounts for the quasi-likelihood:
\[
\text{QIC}(R) = -2 \hat{Q}(\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}; I) + 2 \text{tr}(\hat{\boldsymbol{\Omega}}_I \hat{\mathbf{V}}_R)
\]
where the independence model quasi-likelihood is used as the reference, and \(\hat{\mathbf{V}}_R\) is the sandwich variance under working correlation \(R\).







The Poisson distribution assumes \(\text{Var}(Y) = E[Y] = \mu\).
For tick count data, this is almost never true: - Spatial heterogeneity in plots → some plots consistently have very high or low tick counts - Temporal aggregation → tick counts spike during peak season - Biological clustering → ticks aggregate in microhabitats



The negative binomial distribution with parameters \(\mu\) and \(\theta\) has: \[
\text{Var}(Y) = \mu + \frac{\mu^2}{\theta}
\]
As \(\theta \to \infty\), NB → Poisson. For small \(\theta\) (high overdispersion), variance much exceeds the mean.

                family = poisson, data = tick_data)
# Check: residual deviance / df
fit_pois$deviance / fit_pois$df.residual
# Values >> 1 indicate overdispersion

title: 1 Expanded Lecture Notes: GEE for Repeated-Measures Count Data
text:
The distinction between marginal and conditional models is foundational.
Conditional model (GLMM): \[
\log(E[Y_{it} \mid b_i]) = \mathbf{X}_{it}^\top \boldsymbol{\beta} + b_i
\] where \(b_i \sim N(0, \sigma^2)\) is a random effect for plot \(i\).
The interpretation of \(\boldsymbol{\beta}\) here is: the effect of a one-unit change in a covariate on the log-expected tick count for a plot with random effect \(b_i\). The coefficient is plot-specific.
Marginal model (GEE): \[
\log(E[Y_{it}]) = \mathbf{X}_{it}^\top \boldsymbol{\beta}
\]
Here \(\boldsymbol{\beta}\) is the population-averaged effect — the average effect across all plots.



For the tick burning question: “Does prescribed burning reduce tick abundance on average across the landscape?” — the marginal GEE answer is directly what we want. If we asked “what is the tick reduction in plot 7 specifically?”, GLMM would be more appropriate.
In large-cohort observational studies (where GEE originated in biostatistics), the population-averaged effect is almost always the policy-relevant quantity.

title: 1 Expanded Lecture Notes: GEE for Repeated-Measures Count Data
text:
Let: - \(K\) = number of clusters (21 plots) - \(n_i\) = number of observations in cluster \(i\) (up to 24 months) - \(Y_{it}\) = tick count for plot \(i\) at month \(t\) - \(\mu_{it} = E[Y_{it} \mid \mathbf{X}_{it}]\) = marginal mean - \(\mathbf{Y}_i = (Y_{i1}, \ldots, Y_{in_i})^\top\), \(\boldsymbol{\mu}_i = (\mu_{i1}, \ldots, \mu_{in_i})^\top\)



For a generalized linear model (GLM) with response vector \(\mathbf{Y}_i\) and mean \(\boldsymbol{\mu}_i\), the quasi-likelihood score equation for a single cluster would be:
\[
\mathbf{S}_i(\boldsymbol{\beta}) = \mathbf{D}_i^\top \mathbf{V}_i^{-1} (\mathbf{Y}_i - \boldsymbol{\mu}_i)
\]
where: - \(\mathbf{D}_i = \frac{\partial \boldsymbol{\mu}_i}{\partial \boldsymbol{\beta}}\) is the \(n_i \times p\) Jacobian of the mean w.r.t. parameters - \(\mathbf{V}_i\) is the working covariance matrix of \(\mathbf{Y}_i\)
Summing over all clusters and setting equal to zero gives the GEE:
\[
\sum_{i=1}^{K} \mathbf{D}_i^\top \mathbf{V}_i^{-1} (\mathbf{Y}_i - \boldsymbol{\mu}_i) = \mathbf{0}
\]



\[
\mathbf{V}_i = \phi \, \mathbf{A}_i^{1/2} \, R(\alpha) \, \mathbf{A}_i^{1/2}
\]
where: - \(\phi\) = dispersion parameter (estimated from data; for negative binomial, this is the overdispersion) - \(\mathbf{A}_i\) = diagonal matrix with \(\text{Var}(\mu_{it})\) on the diagonal (depends on the family; for Poisson: \(\mu_{it}\); for negative binomial: \(\mu_{it} + \mu_{it}^2/\theta\)) - \(R(\alpha)\) = working correlation matrix



Liang & Zeger (1986) showed that if the mean structure \(\boldsymbol{\mu}_i = h(\mathbf{X}_i \boldsymbol{\beta})\) is correctly specified, then \(\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}\) is consistent regardless of whether \(R(\alpha)\) is the true correlation matrix. This is GEE’s defining advantage.

title: 1 Expanded Lecture Notes: GEE for Repeated-Measures Count Data
text:
The GEE estimator \(\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}\) solves \(\sum_i \mathbf{S}_i(\boldsymbol{\beta}) = \mathbf{0}\).
By a Taylor expansion:
\[
\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}} - \boldsymbol{\beta}_0 \approx \left[\sum_i \frac{\partial \mathbf{S}_i}{\partial \boldsymbol{\beta}}\right]^{-1} \sum_i \mathbf{S}_i(\boldsymbol{\beta}_0)
\]
The asymptotic variance of \(\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}\) is:
\[
\text{Var}(\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}) = \mathbf{B}^{-1} \, \text{Var}\!\left(\sum_i \mathbf{S}_i\right) \mathbf{B}^{-1}
\]
where \(\mathbf{B} = \sum_i \mathbf{D}_i^\top \mathbf{V}_i^{-1} \mathbf{D}_i\) (the bread).
Since the clusters are independent, \(\text{Var}(\sum_i \mathbf{S}_i) = \sum_i \text{Var}(\mathbf{S}_i)\).
Estimating \(\text{Var}(\mathbf{S}_i)\) empirically gives the meat:
\[
\mathbf{M} = \sum_i \mathbf{D}_i^\top \mathbf{V}_i^{-1} \widehat{\text{Cov}}(\mathbf{Y}_i) \mathbf{V}_i^{-1} \mathbf{D}_i
\]
where \(\widehat{\text{Cov}}(\mathbf{Y}_i) = (\mathbf{Y}_i - \hat{\boldsymbol{\mu}}_i)(\mathbf{Y}_i - \hat{\boldsymbol{\mu}}_i)^\top\).
The sandwich estimator is:
\[
\widehat{\text{Var}}(\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}) = \mathbf{B}^{-1} \mathbf{M} \mathbf{B}^{-1}
\]



The sandwich estimator assumes \(K \to \infty\). With \(K = 21\) clusters (as in this paper), it can be anti-conservative. Corrections include: - Jackknife SE: std.err = "jack" in geepack::geeglm - Pan (2001) small-sample correction - Bootstrap SEs (resample entire clusters)

title: 1 Expanded Lecture Notes: GEE for Repeated-Measures Count Data
text:
Gleim et al. (2014) state (paraphrasing from Methods, p. 3):

“We used generalized estimating equations (GEE) with a negative binomial distribution and log link function to account for the repeated monthly sampling within plots. The exchangeable working correlation structure was used.”

Predictors mentioned in the text include: burn treatment (BB, BUB, UBB, UBUB), host abundance indices (trail camera data), vegetation structure (canopy closure, tree density, ground cover), and climatic variables.

title: 1 Formative Quiz: Generalized Estimating Equations (GEE)
text:
SNR 690 — Contemporary Approaches to Quantitative Methods
Topic: GEE for Repeated-Measures Count Data (Gleim et al. 2014)
This quiz is formative — for learning and self-assessment, not for grades. Attempt each question before reading the answer.



A researcher has 30 forest plots. Each plot is sampled monthly for 18 months for bird abundance (count data). She wants to know whether habitat type (logged vs. unlogged) affects average bird abundance across all plots.
Which approach is most appropriate and why?

Repeated-measures ANOVA — handles the repeated monthly observations within plots.

Generalized Linear Mixed Model (GLMM) — provides cluster-specific (conditional) effects of habitat type on each plot.

Generalized Estimating Equations (GEE) — provides population-averaged (marginal) effect of habitat type across all plots; directly answers the research question.

Standard Poisson GLM with robust SEs — sufficient because habitat type is a between-plot variable.

Correct answer: C
Explanation: The research question asks about the average effect of habitat type across all plots — a population-averaged (marginal) question. GEE is designed for exactly this: it models the marginal mean E[Y] and produces population-averaged coefficients. GLMM (B) would give a conditional effect — the effect for a specific plot with random effect b_i = 0 — which is a subtly different quantity. For a non-linear link (like log for counts), marginal and conditional effects are numerically different. Standard Poisson GLM (D) ignores the within-plot repeated measures correlation, inflating Type I error.




In a GEE with exchangeable working correlation, the analyst suspects the true within-cluster correlation is actually AR(1), not exchangeable. She reports robust (sandwich) standard errors.
What is the effect of this misspecification on her inference?

Her β̂ estimates are biased because the working covariance is wrong.

Her β̂ estimates are consistent (unbiased), but her sandwich SEs are invalid because they assume exchangeable correlation is correct.

Her β̂ estimates are consistent, and her sandwich SEs are valid regardless of the working correlation misspecification — provided the number of clusters is large enough.

She must re-fit the model with the correct AR(1) correlation to get valid estimates and SEs.

Correct answer: C
Explanation: This is GEE’s central advantage. The estimating equation Σ D_i’ V_i⁻¹ (Y_i - μ_i) = 0 gives consistent estimates as long as the mean model (link + family + predictors) is correctly specified — regardless of whether R(α) is right. The sandwich SE Var̂(β̂) = B⁻¹MB⁻¹ is also consistent under misspecification because the “meat” M uses empirical residuals, not model-assumed residuals. The caveat: this requires K → ∞. With small K (e.g., K = 21 in Gleim et al.), the sandwich estimator can be anti-conservative.




A researcher samples 50 soil plots monthly for 12 months, measuring nitrogen concentration (continuous). She expects that measurements in adjacent months are more correlated than measurements 6 months apart.
Which working correlation structure is most ecologically reasonable for her data?

Independence — GEE is robust to misspecification anyway, so it doesn’t matter.

AR(1) — assumes correlation decays with lag; appropriate when adjacent observations are more correlated than distant ones.

Exchangeable — assumes equal correlation between all pairs of time points; appropriate for clustered data without temporal ordering.

Unstructured — estimates all pairwise correlations separately; always most accurate.

Correct answer: B
Explanation: AR(1) specifies Corr(Y_t, Y_s) = α^|t-s|, which decays as the lag |t-s| increases. This directly matches the researcher’s expectation that adjacent months are more correlated. Exchangeable (C) assumes equal correlation at all lags — unrealistic for time-series data with seasonal patterns. Independence (A) ignores correlation entirely; while GEE estimates remain consistent, efficiency is reduced. Unstructured (D) is flexible but requires estimating n(n-1)/2 correlation parameters — with 12 time points that is 66 parameters, which may be unstable and is not available in all GEE implementations.




Prompt: Write the GEE estimating equation and define the working covariance matrix V_i. Identify each component.
Answer:
The GEE estimating equation is:
\[\sum_{i=1}^{K} \mathbf{D}_i^\top \mathbf{V}_i^{-1}(\mathbf{Y}_i - \boldsymbol{\mu}_i) = \mathbf{0}\]
where:



Symbol
Definition




K
Number of clusters (independent sampling units)


Y_i
Observed response vector for cluster i (length n_i)


μ_i
Vector of marginal means: μ_it = E[Y_it | X_it] = h(X_it β)


D_i
Jacobian matrix: D_i = ∂μ_i/∂β (n_i × p matrix)


V_i
Working covariance matrix (see below)



The working covariance matrix:
\[\mathbf{V}_i = \phi \, \mathbf{A}_i^{1/2} \, R(\alpha) \, \mathbf{A}_i^{1/2}\]

φ: dispersion parameter (estimated from data)
A_i: diagonal matrix with Var(μ_it) on the diagonal (depends on family; for Poisson: μ_it; for negative binomial: μ_it + μ_it²/θ)
R(α): working correlation matrix (exchangeable, AR(1), or independence)

Key property: GEE estimates β̂ are consistent if the mean structure is correctly specified, regardless of whether R(α) is the true correlation matrix.




Prompt: A GEE model (negative binomial family, log link, exchangeable correlation) is fit to monthly tick count data from 21 plots in four burn regimes. The output shows:
                   Estimate  Std.err  Wald   Pr(>|W|)
(Intercept)          3.451    0.312  122.1   <0.001
burn_regimeBUB      -0.693    0.178   15.2   <0.001
burn_regimeBB       -1.204    0.201   35.9   <0.001
burn_regimeUBB      -0.511    0.163    9.8    0.002
season_peak          0.875    0.134   42.6   <0.001
Reference category: UBUB (unburned/unburned). Standard errors are robust (sandwich).
Interpret the coefficient for burn_regimeBB in plain language.
Answer:
The coefficient for burn_regimeBB is −1.204 on the log scale.
Step 1: Exponentiate to get the incidence rate ratio (IRR):
exp(−1.204) ≈ 0.30
Step 2: Interpret as a multiplicative change relative to the reference:
Burned/burned (BB) plots have, on average, approximately 70% fewer ticks than unburned/unburned (UBUB) plots (IRR = 0.30; robust 95% CI: approximately 0.20–0.45), holding season and other covariates constant.
Step 3: Emphasize the marginal interpretation (because this is GEE):
This is a population-averaged effect: averaged across all 21 plots and all time points in the study, BB plots have 70% fewer ticks than UBUB plots. This is not the effect for any specific plot.
Step 4: The robust (sandwich) SEs account for the within-plot temporal correlation, giving valid inference even if the exchangeable working correlation is misspecified.

Aligned to Learning Objectives: Q1→LO1, Q2→LO2+LO5, Q3→LO3, Q4→LO2, Q5→LO5

μ_i

D_i
Jacobian matrix: D_i = ∂μ_i/∂β (n_i × p matrix)


V_i
Working covariance matrix (see below)



The working covariance matrix:
\[\mathbf{V}_i = \phi \, \mathbf{A}_i^{1/2} \, R(\alpha) \, \mathbf{A}_i^{1/2}\]

φ: dispersion parameter (estimated from data)
A_i: diagonal matrix with Var(μ_it) on the diagonal (depends on family; for Poisson: μ_it; for negative binomial: μ_it + μ_it²/θ)
R(α): working correlation matrix (exchangeable, AR(1), or independence)

Key property: GEE estimates β̂ are consistent if the mean structure is correctly specified, regardless of whether R(α) is the true correlation matrix.

title: 1 Formative Quiz: Generalized Estimating Equations (GEE)
text:
Prompt: A GEE model (negative binomial family, log link, exchangeable correlation) is fit to monthly tick count data from 21 plots in four burn regimes. The output shows:
                   Estimate  Std.err  Wald   Pr(>|W|)
(Intercept)          3.451    0.312  122.1   <0.001
burn_regimeBUB      -0.693    0.178   15.2   <0.001
burn_regimeBB       -1.204    0.201   35.9   <0.001
burn_regimeUBB      -0.511    0.163    9.8    0.002
season_peak          0.875    0.134   42.6   <0.001
Reference category: UBUB (unburned/unburned). Standard errors are robust (sandwich).
Interpret the coefficient for burn_regimeBB in plain language.
Answer:
The coefficient for burn_regimeBB is −1.204 on the log scale.
Step 1: Exponentiate to get the incidence rate ratio (IRR):
exp(−1.204) ≈ 0.30
Step 2: Interpret as a multiplicative change relative to the reference:
Burned/burned (BB) plots have, on average, approximately 70% fewer ticks than unburned/unburned (UBUB) plots (IRR = 0.30; robust 95% CI: approximately 0.20–0.45), holding season and other covariates constant.
Step 3: Emphasize the marginal interpretation (because this is GEE):
This is a population-averaged effect: averaged across all 21 plots and all time points in the study, BB plots have 70% fewer ticks than UBUB plots. This is not the effect for any specific plot.
Step 4: The robust (sandwich) SEs account for the within-plot temporal correlation, giving valid inference even if the exchangeable working correlation is misspecified.

Aligned to Learning Objectives: Q1→LO1, Q2→LO2+LO5, Q3→LO3, Q4→LO2, Q5→LO5

title: 1 Peer Review Rubric: Methods Paper Analysis
text:
SNR 690 — Contemporary Approaches to Quantitative Methods
Reviewer name: ___________________________________

Score each criterion on a 1–4 scale using the descriptors below. Provide specific written comments for each criterion. The goal is constructive, specific feedback — not a general rating.




Does the presenter/author correctly describe and justify the statistical methods used in the paper?







Score
Descriptor




4 — Exemplary
Accurately identifies the GEE model specification (family, link, correlation structure), explains why each choice is appropriate for the data structure, and identifies at least one defensible alternative. Correctly distinguishes GEE from GLMM.


3 — Proficient
Accurately identifies most of the model specification and provides a reasonable justification for the main choices. Minor omissions (e.g., does not mention AR(1) alternative).


2 — Developing
Identifies the general method (GEE) but misidentifies or omits key components (e.g., does not mention negative binomial, or confuses clusters with individual observations).

Does the presenter/author correctly interpret the model results in the context of the research question?







Score
Descriptor

Correctly interprets coefficients on the log scale (IRR = exp(β)); connects results to the biological question (“prescribed burning reduces tick abundance by X% on average”); acknowledges uncertainty (CIs, robust SEs). Distinguishes marginal from conditional effects if applicable.


3 — Proficient
Correctly interprets direction of effects and connects to the research question. May not fully articulate the multiplicative interpretation (exp(β)) or the role of robust SEs.


2 — Developing
States whether effects are “significant” but does not quantify the magnitude or connect to the biological question. May confuse log-scale and original-scale interpretation.

Provides (or explicitly acknowledges the paper’s lack of) a formal model equation; lists all predictors; mentions software and package; notes that raw data are not publicly available and proposes a simulation strategy. Code (if provided) runs without error.

Lists main predictors and software; acknowledges data availability. Code (if provided) runs with minor modifications. Does not explicitly note the absence of a formal equation in the paper.

title: 1 Minute Paper
text:
SNR 690 — Contemporary Approaches to Quantitative Methods
Topic: GEE for Repeated-Measures Count Data (Gleim et al. 2014)
Time: 3 minutes | Submission: Index card OR Canvas assignment



1. What is the single most important thing you learned about GEE today?
Be specific. “I learned about GEE” is not acceptable. Example of a good answer: “I learned that GEE gives population-averaged (marginal) effects, not plot-specific effects, which is what you want if your question is about average treatment effects across a landscape.”
 
 
 
 
 

2. What is one question you still have about applying GEE to your own research data?
Connect this to your actual research context. Examples: “I’m not sure whether my 15 field sites are enough clusters for the sandwich SE to be reliable.” or “I have count data with lots of zeros — would I use zero-inflated GEE or quasi-Poisson?”
 
 
 
 
 





In-class: Write on the back of this card or a blank index card. Hand in before you leave.
Digital: Submit via Canvas (assignment: “Minute Paper — GEE Week”) by end of day.
Anonymous option: If you prefer, you do not need to write your name. However, if you want follow-up, include your name or a pseudonym.





Research in cognitive science (e.g., Bransford et al., How People Learn, 2000) consistently shows that brief reflective writing immediately after learning strengthens retention and transfer. Question 2 specifically asks you to transfer today’s methods to your own context — the hardest and most important cognitive step.
Your instructor reads all minute papers before the next class session and will address recurring questions at the start of the next meeting.

title: 1 Group Worksheet: Critiquing a GEE Analysis
text:
SNR 690 — Contemporary Approaches to Quantitative Methods
Paper: Gleim et al. (2014). Tick phenology & prescribed burning. PLOS ONE, 9(11), e112174. DOI: 10.1371/journal.pone.0112174
Time limit: 10 minutes | Group size: 3–4 students




Note: Gleim et al. (2014) do not provide an explicit model equation. The specification below is inferred from the Methods section.








Element
Specification




Family
Negative binomial (overdispersed counts)


Link
Log


Correlation structure
Exchangeable


Clusters
21 sampling plots


Repeated measures
Monthly sampling over 2 years (up to 24 time points per plot)


Predictor(s)
Burn regime (BB, BUB, UBB, UBUB), host abundance, canopy closure, tree density, ground cover, temperature, precipitation



Inferred equation:
log(E[ticks_it]) = β₀ + β₁·BurnRegime_i + β₂·HostAbundance_it + β₃·Canopy_it + β₄·Season_it + …




Answer as many as you can in 10 minutes. Your group’s main deliverable is prompt 7 (the challenge question).
1. Family choice: Is negative binomial appropriate?
What is overdispersion, and how would you check for it? What alternatives exist (Poisson, quasi-Poisson, zero-inflated)? Under what conditions would you prefer each?
Your group’s answer:
 
 

2. Correlation structure: Is exchangeable realistic for monthly ecological data?
For a 24-month time series, exchangeable correlation assumes Corr(Jan, Feb) = Corr(Jan, Dec). Is that biologically realistic? Would AR(1) be more defensible? What is the practical consequence of misspecifying the correlation structure?
Your group’s answer:
 
 

3. Cluster balance: Are the 21 plots balanced across treatments?
21 plots across 4 burn regimes does not divide evenly. How might unequal cluster sizes affect GEE? Does unequal plot area (7.2–78.9 ha) matter? Should plot area be included as a covariate or offset?
Your group’s answer:
 
 

4. Transparency: The paper does not report the full model equation — is this a problem?
What information would you need to fully replicate the analysis? Does the absence of a formal model equation meet the standards for reproducible research? What would you require in a peer review?
Your group’s answer:
 
 

5. Robust SEs: How does the sandwich variance estimator protect against correlation misspecification?
Explain in plain language why GEE point estimates are consistent even if the working correlation is wrong. Under what conditions does the sandwich estimator fail? Is K = 21 clusters sufficient?
Your group’s answer:
 
 

6. GEE vs. GLMM: What would change if they used a GLMM instead?
Describe one practical difference in how you would interpret the burn regime coefficient from a GEE vs. a GLMM. Which interpretation is more relevant to a land manager deciding whether to prescribe burn?
Your group’s answer:
 
 




Write one methodological question that you want the presenter to answer. This should be a question that requires genuine engagement with the paper’s methods — not a factual recall question.

Our group’s challenge question:
 
 
 


Learning objectives addressed: LO 1, 2, 3, 4 (critique), 5 (implementation)
Instructor: Collect challenge questions at 10 min; call on 2–3 groups during group reports (1:05–1:12)

title: Instructor Notes — Tick Phenology & Prescribed Burning: GEE for Repeated-Measures Count Data
text:
For Instructor Use Only



This document is not for distribution to students. It provides step-by-step facilitation guidance, expected student difficulties, contingency plans, and follow-up assignments.

title: Instructor Notes — Tick Phenology & Prescribed Burning: GEE for Repeated-Measures Count Data
text:
1.1 Pre-Class Setup (~15 min before)

Distribute (or post on Canvas) activities/pre_activity_entry_ticket.csv so students have it ready at the start.
Open slides/slides-ticks-gee.qmd in your browser — advance past the title slide to Slide 3 (Entry Ticket Debrief prompts) so it is ready when you walk in.
Write the Hot-Seat Q&A instructions on the board (or keep Slide 9 ready): “Your group has 60 seconds to write one question for the presenter. The presenter will answer on the spot.”
Confirm that the presenter knows the Hot-Seat is coming at ~20 min into their presentation (tell them at the start or remind them the day before).
If students will run R code live, verify geepack and MASS are installed in the classroom R environment.

title: Instructor Notes — Tick Phenology & Prescribed Burning: GEE for Repeated-Measures Count Data
text:
1.2 Minute-by-Minute Facilitation Guide

1.2.1 0:00–0:05 — Entry Ticket (5 min)

Project the entry ticket fields on the screen (Slide 3) or tell students to open the CSV.
Say: “Five minutes — paper out, answer these. Be honest; this is not graded.”
Walk around and read over students’ shoulders. Mentally note:

Did students identify plots (not individual ticks) as the clusters?
Did anyone mention GEE, repeated measures, or clustering?
Any especially good or creative method questions?





1.2.2 0:05–0:10 — Entry Ticket Debrief (5 min)

Summarize verbally: “I saw a few people say [X]. That’s exactly right because…” and “Several of you wrote [Y] — let’s come back to that during the lecture.”
Key points to reinforce:

Cluster = plot (not individual ticks, not year)
Outcome = count data (overdispersed → negative binomial is defensible)
GEE is appropriate when you want marginal (population-averaged) effects








Tip



Facilitation tip: Don’t give away the full lecture yet. Use this 5 minutes to whet curiosity. Say: “There’s a really interesting tension between GEE and GLMM for this design — we’ll get to that.”





1.2.3 0:10–0:40 — Student Presentation (30 min)
Your role: Active moderator, not passive audience.

At minute 12–15 (before the Hot-Seat), listen for whether the presenter:

Correctly identifies the cluster structure
Mentions overdispersion or count data
Explains what GEE does (or tries to)

At approximately 20 minutes into the presentation, interrupt politely: “Great — let’s pause here for our Hot-Seat Q&A round.”


1.2.3.1 Facilitating the Hot-Seat (5 min within the presentation)

Announce: “Groups, you have 60 seconds to write one question for [presenter’s name]. This is a question they must answer right now.”
Set a visible timer (phone or projector).
Call on 2–3 groups (time permitting): “Group 1, what’s your question?”
After each question, say: “[Presenter], you have 30 seconds.”
If the presenter struggles, do NOT immediately rescue — wait 5 seconds, then offer: “Anyone want to help [presenter] out?”, and then ask the presenter to synthesize.
After the Hot-Seat, let the presenter finish their remaining ~10 minutes.







Warning



Common presenter mistake: Trying to answer questions with “I think…” followed by a restatement of the paper. Encourage them to use their own words and to explicitly say “I don’t know, but I would look at…” if they are uncertain.






1.2.4 0:40–0:55 — GEE Mini-Lecture (15 min)
Use Slides 4–8. Key teaching points per slide:

Slide 4 (Why GEE?): Emphasize marginal vs conditional interpretation. “GEE answers: what is the average effect of burn treatment across all plots?”
Slide 5 (Estimating Equation): Walk through each symbol slowly. Spend 2–3 min on \(\mathbf{V}_i = \mathbf{A}_i^{1/2} R(\alpha) \mathbf{A}_i^{1/2}\). Key insight: the working correlation does not have to be correct — GEE is consistent even if \(R(\alpha)\) is wrong.
Slide 6 (Sandwich Variance): One sentence: “The sandwich variance is consistent even when the working covariance is wrong — that is GEE’s magic.” Then move on.
Slide 7 (Paper’s Model): Be explicit that the paper does NOT give a formal equation. Show the inferred model and ask: “Does anyone want to challenge this specification?”
Slide 8 (Working Correlation Table): Spend 2 min — AR(1) is usually more realistic for monthly ecological data, but exchangeable is common in practice. This is a good setup for the group worksheet.




1.2.5 0:55–1:05 — Group Worksheet (10 min)

Distribute activities/worksheet_group_ticks.md (or show it on screen).
Give explicit instructions: “You have 10 minutes. Answer as many of the 6 prompts as you can — but your main deliverable is one ‘challenge question’ for [presenter’s name] at the end.”
Circulate. Suggested probing questions when groups get stuck:

“Would the correlation between tick counts in January and December be the same as between January and February? What does that imply?” (prompts AR(1) vs exchangeable thinking)
“If they had used a GLMM instead, how would you interpret the burn regime coefficient differently?” (marginal vs conditional)
“The paper says ‘negative binomial GEE’ — but geepack::geeglm doesn’t have a negative binomial family option. What does that tell you about the software?”





1.2.6 1:05–1:12 — Group Reports + Challenge Questions (7 min)

Call on 2–3 groups: “What is your group’s challenge question for [presenter]?”
After each question, give the presenter 45–60 seconds to respond.
If responses are weak, ask follow-up: “[Presenter], do you want to revise that answer?”
Briefly synthesize after all groups: “Here’s what I want you to take away from these questions…”







Tip



If time is tight: Skip to the most conceptually rich challenge question (usually the GEE vs GLMM one or the AR(1) vs exchangeable one).





1.2.7 1:12–1:15 — Minute Paper (3 min)

Project Slide 11 (Minute Paper prompts).
Say: “Two questions, index card or phone. You have 3 minutes. Be specific.”
Collect before students leave. Review before next class.

title: Instructor Notes — Tick Phenology & Prescribed Burning: GEE for Repeated-Measures Count Data
text:
2.1 1. Confusing GEE with GLMM
The confusion: Both handle clustered/repeated-measures data and both can use non-normal link functions. Students often use the terms interchangeably.
How to address: - Key distinction: GEE models the marginal mean (population-averaged effect); GLMM models the conditional mean (cluster-specific effect). - Example: “The effect of burn treatment on tick counts across all plots (GEE) vs. within a specific plot (GLMM).” - Analogy: GEE is like asking “on average, do treated fields have fewer ticks?” GLMM asks “for plot #7 specifically, do treated conditions reduce ticks?”
Relevant worksheet prompt: Question 6.

title: Instructor Notes — Tick Phenology & Prescribed Burning: GEE for Repeated-Measures Count Data
text:
2.2 2. Sandwich SEs vs. Model-Based SEs
The confusion: Students don’t understand why you’d use robust SEs instead of the model-based ones — “aren’t model-based SEs better if the model is correct?”
How to address: - In practice, the working correlation is never exactly right. Sandwich SEs are consistent regardless. - If the working covariance is correctly specified, sandwich and model-based SEs converge. - Analogy: Model-based SEs are like a GPS that assumes perfect roads; sandwich SEs are like a GPS that recalculates based on actual traffic.
Relevant worksheet prompt: Question 5.

title: Instructor Notes — Tick Phenology & Prescribed Burning: GEE for Repeated-Measures Count Data
section: 2.3 3. Negative Binomial vs. Poisson Family Choice
text:
2.3 3. Negative Binomial vs. Poisson Family Choice
The confusion: Students may not understand what overdispersion means or how to detect it.
How to address: - For count data, Poisson assumes Var(Y) = E(Y). If Var(Y) > E(Y), the data are overdispersed. - Signs of overdispersion: residual deviance/df >> 1 in a Poisson GLM; estimated dispersion parameter > 1. - geepack::geeglm uses a quasi-Poisson approach (scale parameter estimated from data); true negative binomial GEE requires additional packages.
Relevant R script: materials/gee_example.R section “Overdispersion Check.”

title: Instructor Notes — Tick Phenology & Prescribed Burning: GEE for Repeated-Measures Count Data
text:
2.4 4. Not Recognizing the Cluster Structure
The confusion: Students may identify individual tick observations as the unit of analysis, or confuse the cluster (plot) with the treatment (burn regime).
How to address: - Draw the data structure on the board: Plot 1 (BB)   → Jan, Feb, Mar, ... Dec 2010, Jan, ..., Dec 2011   Plot 2 (BB)   → Jan, Feb, Mar, ... Dec 2010, Jan, ..., Dec 2011   ...   Plot 21 (UBUB) → ... - Key point: plots are the independent units; monthly observations within a plot are dependent (correlated over time).
Relevant entry ticket field: Cluster_ID_and_time_structure.

title: Instructor Notes — Tick Phenology & Prescribed Burning: GEE for Repeated-Measures Count Data
text:
2.5 5. Working Correlation Choice (AR(1) vs. Exchangeable)
The confusion: Students may think exchangeable is “fine” because GEE is robust to misspecification.
How to address: - GEE estimates are consistent regardless of working correlation — but efficiency (precision) depends on how close the working correlation is to the truth. - Monthly tick counts likely have autocorrelation that decays with lag — AR(1) is more ecologically realistic. - For a 24-time-point monthly series, assuming equal correlation between January and December (exchangeable) as between consecutive months is probably wrong.

title: Instructor Notes — Tick Phenology & Prescribed Burning: GEE for Repeated-Measures Count Data
text:
3.1 Managing the Hot-Seat

Rehearse this the day before by telling the presenter: “At about minute 20, I’ll call a Hot-Seat. Groups will ask you a question. You’ll have 30 seconds per question. Don’t panic — say what you know.”
Frame it as professional development: “This is what happens at conference Q&A and dissertation defenses.”
If a group’s question is unclear or unfair, rephrase it: “I think what they’re asking is…”
If the presenter gives a great answer, say so explicitly: “That was a really precise answer — did everyone catch what they said?”

title: Instructor Notes — Tick Phenology & Prescribed Burning: GEE for Repeated-Measures Count Data
text:
3.2 Managing Challenge Questions from Groups

Encourage groups to write genuinely challenging questions, not softballs. During the worksheet phase, if you see a group writing an easy question, push back: “What’s a question that would make a methodologist sweat a bit?”
After the presenter responds, ask the questioning group: “Does that answer satisfy you? Anything you’d add?” This creates a mini-dialogue.

title: Instructor Notes — Tick Phenology & Prescribed Burning: GEE for Repeated-Measures Count Data
section: 3.3 If the Presenter Doesn’t Know the Answer
text:
3.3 If the Presenter Doesn’t Know the Answer

Don’t rescue immediately. Wait 5–8 seconds.
Offer: “What would you need to look at to figure that out?”
If truly stumped, open to the class: “Who wants to help?”
Return to the presenter for a synthesis: “Based on what the class just said, how would you revise your answer?”

title: Instructor Notes — Tick Phenology & Prescribed Burning: GEE for Repeated-Measures Count Data
text:
4.1 Option A: R Modification (recommended)

title: Week 08 — Lecture Notes: The Philosophy Behind Your Analysis
text:
Instructor Use Only



These are structured talking points for the ~20-minute mini-lecture in Session A, Part 2. They are not distributed to students in advance.

title: Week 08 — Lecture Notes: The Philosophy Behind Your Analysis
text:
1.1 Opening Hook (2 min)

“Imagine you’re in your dissertation defense. Your committee asks: ‘Why did you use this method?’ What do you say?”

Pause. Let the question land.

“If your answer is ‘because my advisor does it that way’ or ‘because I Googled it and it seemed right’ — that’s a problem. Not because those methods are wrong, but because you can’t defend them. Today we’re going to practice defending them.”

Transition: “Let’s start by revisiting something from Week 2 that sounds abstract but is deeply practical.”

title: Week 08 — Lecture Notes: The Philosophy Behind Your Analysis
text:
1.2 Section 1: Revisiting Box (5 min)
Core idea: “All models are wrong, some are useful.” — George Box
This is not a cop-out or a disclaimer. It is a design principle.
What it means in practice:

Your model doesn’t need to perfectly describe reality. It needs to be wrong in a way that doesn’t matter for your specific question.

Two examples of being wrong in a way that DOES matter:






Example 1: Linear model on count data



lm(insect_count ~ treatment)
Count data cannot be negative. A linear model can predict negative counts. When your counts are small (0–10), this matters — your inference is compromised.
Wrong in a way that matters.








Example 2: RCBD ANOVA on a split-plot design



aov(yield ~ variety + block) — ignoring that sowing time is a whole-plot factor.
You are using the wrong error term for the whole-plot factor. Your F-ratio for sowing time is anticonservative (too many false positives).
Wrong in a way that matters. (See Week 5.)


Actionable version for students:

Ask yourself: “In what way is my model wrong? Does that wrongness affect my conclusions?”

If you can answer that honestly, you are doing philosophy.

title: Week 08 — Lecture Notes: The Philosophy Behind Your Analysis
section: 1.3 Section 2: What Kind of Inference? (5 min)
text:
1.3 Section 2: What Kind of Inference? (5 min)
Core idea: Your method must match the type of claim you want to make.









Type
What you’re doing
What you need
Example




Descriptive
Summarizing what is in your data
Representative sample, appropriate summary stats
“Mean wheat yield was 4.2 Mg/ha across all sites”


Predictive
Forecasting new observations
Cross-validation, held-out test set
“A model trained on 2020–2022 predicts 2023 yield within 0.3 Mg/ha”


Causal
Claiming X causes Y
Randomization or strong causal design + assumptions
“Nitrogen application increased yield by 0.8 Mg/ha” (from RCT)









Warning: Observational Data + Regression ≠ Causal



This is one of the most common errors in applied agricultural and ecological research.

“We found that farms with higher biodiversity had lower pest pressure (β = −0.42, p < 0.001). Therefore, increasing biodiversity reduces pests.”

This is a causal claim from an observational study. The regression tells you there is an association. It does not tell you why.
You need a randomized experiment, a natural experiment, or explicit causal modeling (DAGs, instrumental variables) to make causal claims.


Transition: “So: what kind of inference are you making? Point at your Methods section mentally. Does your method actually support that type of claim?”

title: Week 08 — Lecture Notes: The Philosophy Behind Your Analysis
section: 1.4 Section 3: The Triangle in Practice (5 min)
text:
1.4 Section 3: The Triangle in Practice (5 min)
Core idea: Question + Data + Method must all align. (Week 3 recap, with new examples.)
Three concrete examples from ag / natural resources:






Good Alignment: Nitrogen Rate × Year on Wheat Yield




Question: Does nitrogen rate affect wheat yield, and does this vary by year?
Data: Continuous yield (kg/ha); blocked RCT; two years; plots within blocks
Method: lmer(yield ~ N_rate * Year + (1|Block), data = dat)
Triangle: ✅ Continuous response → lmer; blocked design → random block; factorial treatment → interaction term

This is defensible.








Common Mistake: Bird Richness Counts with Repeated Measures → aov()




Question: Is bird species richness declining over years across urban parks?
Data: Integer counts (0–47); 8 parks; 6 visits/year; 5 years; counts are non-normal
Method used: aov(richness ~ year)
Triangle: ❌ Count data → Poisson, not Gaussian; repeated measures → random effects needed; pseudoreplication ignored

Should be: glmer(richness ~ year + (1|park), family = poisson, data = dat)








Overcomplicated: Soil pH, 2 Groups, 10 Samples → Bayesian Hierarchical Model




Question: Does tillage treatment affect soil pH?
Data: 10 samples per treatment (n = 20 total); continuous pH; no nesting or grouping structure
Method used: Full Bayesian hierarchical model with weakly informative priors and MCMC
Triangle: ⚠️ A t-test (or simple Bayesian two-sample comparison) is sufficient. The added complexity provides no additional insight and makes your methods section impenetrable.

Complexity must be earned by your data, not borrowed from methodology papers.

title: Week 08 — Lecture Notes: The Philosophy Behind Your Analysis
text:
1.5 Section 4: Tying It Together (3 min)
Core message:

“Your methods section is not just a technical description — it is a philosophical argument.”

When a reviewer reads your methods, they are asking:

Did you know what kind of claim you were making?
Did you choose a method that is honest about what your data can support?
Did you avoid being either naively simple or gratuitously complex?

Transition to Think-Pair-Share:

“Now you’re going to make that argument — out loud, to a peer who will push back. Take out a notecard.”

title: Week 08 — Lecture Notes: The Philosophy Behind Your Analysis
text:

title: Final Paper: Project Home
text:
4 Step 3: Modify the README
Edit your README.md file to include the following sections:

4.1 Objective
What do you aim to accomplish with this research?


4.2 Research Question
What specific question are you trying to answer?


4.3 Variables
List your response variable(s) and predictor variable(s)


4.4 Experimental Design or Data Structure
Describe your data collection approach or existing dataset structure


4.5 Analysis Method
What statistical method(s) will you use?


4.6 Main Result
What do you expect to find or demonstrate?


4.7 Logical Alignment
Do these components align logically? Explain briefly how your methods will answer your research question.


4.8 Expected Timeline
Create a week-by-week plan from Week 6 through Week 14 outlining your anticipated progress on the final paper. For each week, write a brief description of what you plan to accomplish (e.g., data cleaning, running analyses, drafting the methods section). This timeline should be realistic and account for your other coursework and obligations. You will use this as a self-assessment tool throughout the semester: revisiting and updating it as you progress.


4.9 Usefulness for Your Career or Degree
Briefly describe how this project connects to your thesis or your broader academic or professional goals. How does working through this research question and analysis contribute to your development as a researcher or practitioner in your field? Does it help you with your thesis/dissertation? This section should demonstrate that the project is not just an academic exercise, but an opportunity to apply appropriate quantitative approaches to a problem that is meaningful and relevant to your own research trajectory

title: Final Paper: Project Home
section: 6 Step 5: Write a method justification section
text:
6 Step 5: Write a method justification section
On the same document, write a brief justification for your chosen method(s). This should start with a sentence like this: - I chose X methods because … - A key limitation of my analysis is … so we … - Write down a description of the analysis you will do, and start working on it! At this point you should start coding
It should be two or so paragraphs.
Your paragraphs can address:






TipWriting Checklist



⬜ What is your response variable and what distribution does it follow?
⬜ What is your data structure (independent, nested, repeated, blocked)?
⬜ Why is a simpler analysis insufficient?
⬜ Why is a more complex analysis not warranted?
⬜ What assumptions does your method make? List ALL OF THEM!


And more, depending on your project. The goal is to justify your method choice and acknowledge its limitations. And to be 100% sure about it befor you go into data cleaning and analysis.

title: Final Paper: Project Home
text:
7 Step 6: Check your assumptions
Based on the assumptions you wrote, check your assumptions. You can do this by hand, on a piece of paper or on your project. list every assumption your chosen model requires. Then rate each one:







Assumption
Status




(e.g., independence of residuals)
🟢 Checked: and it holds


(e.g., normality of random effects)
🟡 Haven’t checked yet: but I could by next class


(e.g., no spatial autocorrelation)
🔴 Can’t fully check: need to acknowledge it and discuss it



The goal is not to have all green. Red assumptions are ok! they are honest limitations (all studies have them) that belong in your discussion section.

title: Final Paper: Project Home
text:
9 Example Project Template






Important



By the end of this week your project should look like this example (obviously with more detail and specific to your project)!


Objective: Assess the impact of habitat restoration on native bird species richness in riparian zones
Research Question: Does riparian habitat restoration increase native bird species richness compared to unrestored sites?
Variables: - Response: Bird species richness (count) - Predictors: Restoration status (restored/unrestored), time since restoration (years), habitat area (hectares)
Experimental Design or Data Structure: Observational study with 20 restored and 20 unrestored riparian sites across Arizona, sampled over 3 breeding seasons
Analysis Method: Generalized Linear Mixed Model (Poisson regression) with site as random effect to account for repeated measures
Main Result: Restored sites are expected to show significantly higher species richness, with effect size increasing with time since restoration
Logical Alignment: Yes - the GLMM approach handles count data (species richness) and accounts for site-level variation while testing the restoration effect. The temporal component allows assessment of restoration trajectory, directly addressing whether and how restoration increases bird diversity.
Expected Timeline: - Week 6: Finalize research question analysis plan - Week 7: Clean data - Week 8: Run preliminary analyses - Week 9: Interpret results and create figures - Week 10: Draft methods section - Week 11: Draft results section - Week 12: Draft introduction and discussion - Week 13: Revise and finalize paper - Week 14: Submit final paper
Usefulness for Your Career or Degree: This project directly informs my thesis research on habitat restoration and biodiversity. It allows me to apply advanced statistical methods to real-world ecological data, enhancing my quantitative skills and contributing to a publishable paper that will strengthen my CV and support my future academic career.
Method Justification: I chose a Generalized Linear Mixed Model with a Poisson distribution because my response variable, bird species richness, is a count variable that typically follows a Poisson distribution. A simpler analysis, such as a t-test comparing restored and unrestored sites, would not account for the repeated measures across multiple seasons or the potential non-independence of observations within sites. On the other hand, a more complex model, like a zero-inflated model, is not warranted given that preliminary data exploration did not indicate an excess of zeros in species richness.
Assumption Check:








Assumption
Status




Independence of residuals
🟢 Checked: and it holds


Normality of random effects
🟡 Haven’t checked yet: but I could by next class


No spatial autocorrelation
🔴 Can’t fully check: need to acknowledge it and discuss it

section: 4 Why Are We Talking About This?
text:
4 Why Are We Talking About This?
In Weeks 1–3, we talked about how to think about data analysis…
. . .

Week 1: Data exploration and inference are separate - but both must be documented

. . .

Week 2: All models are wrong but some are useful - how do you record which ones you tried? In case it is an iterative process

. . .

Week 3: The Method-Question-Data Triangle - when you iterate through model choices, how do you track that evolution?

. . .
Version control answers all of these questions.

title: Version Control, Git & Collaboration
text:
5 Version Control as a tool

It can also be a very good tool to have. I have applied at jobs, that specifically asked for this. In the future, as more collaborative projects deal with complex models, version control will be a must-have skill AI is expanding the ability to code and do data analysis, which will increase potential collaboration –> source control becomes even more important More importantly… if you are doing science, it should be reproducible.

22 The Git Mental Model
Your Computer (Local)                    GitHub (Remote)
┌─────────────────────┐                ┌──────────────────┐
│  Working Directory   │   git push    │                  │
│  (your files)        │ ──────────►   │   Remote Repo    │
│         │            │               │   (GitHub)       │
│    git add           │   git pull    │                  │
│         ▼            │ ◄──────────   │                  │
│  Staging Area        │               └──────────────────┘
│         │            │

└─────────────────────┘

title: Version Control, Git & Collaboration
text:
23 The Workflow in Plain English

You edit files in your Working Directory

. . .

You stage (git add) the changes you want to record

. . .

You commit - creating a snapshot with a message explaining why

. . .

You push to GitHub so others (and future you!) can see it

. . .

You pull to get changes others have made

title: Version Control, Git & Collaboration
section: 24 Git is Not Just for Code
text:
24 Git is Not Just for Code
Git tracks any text file:

.qmd, .R, .csv (small), .bib, .tex, .md

. . .

24.1 What Git is NOT Good For


Large binary files (images, PDFs, Word docs, big datasets)
Files that change constantly in unpredictable ways
Sensitive data (passwords, API keys, PII)


. . .
For large data → .gitignore and Git Large File Storage (LFS)

title: Version Control, Git & Collaboration
text:
25 GitHub ≠ Git







Git
GitHub




The version control system
A website that hosts Git repositories


Runs on your computer
Runs in the cloud


Tracks history locally
Lets you share, collaborate, and back up


Free, open source
Free for public repos; education accounts get extras



. . .
Git is the engine. GitHub is the garage where everyone parks.

5 Why so repetitive?

I am really focusing on some concepts that I think are really important and that I want you to understand deeply
They are the foundation for your understanding of concepts from a philosophical perspective


If you take a class in the stats department, you will learn a lot of VERY TECHNICAL statistical concepts. Not to apply them If you take one of the AG campus courses, you will learn a lot of technical concepts and how to apply them, but you won’t learn the philosophical concepts that are the foundation for understanding when to apply them and how to defend them. This course is designed to give you the philosophical foundation for understanding when to apply methods and how to defend them. We will touch on technical concepts, but the focus is on the philosophy of science and how it applies to your research.

title: From Philosophy to Practice: Defending Your Analysis
text:
6 Quik Questions 🔍
. . .
Raise your hand if you can answer:
. . .

What does the Method–Question–Data Triangle tell us?

. . .

What does Box mean when he says models are “wrong but useful”?

. . .

What is the difference between a causal and a descriptive claim?

. . .

Why does a split-plot need a different error term than a simple ANOVA?

. . .

title: From Philosophy to Practice: Defending Your Analysis
text:
7 📄 Meet Dr. Reyes
You are a peer reviewer for Ecological Applications.
. . .

Title: Nitrogen addition increases aboveground biomass in semi-arid grasslands

. . .

Methods (excerpt): We established 12 plots across 4 ranches in eastern Colorado. Each ranch received one of three nitrogen treatments: control, low (25 kg N/ha), and high (50 kg N/ha). Biomass was harvested at three time points: May, July, and September.
We ran a one-way ANOVA on the September harvest only, as this represented “peak biomass.” We report p = 0.03 and conclude high nitrogen significantly increased biomass.
We selected ANOVA because it is the standard method in grassland ecology and because our dataset was small. We acknowledge data were not fully independent but considered this a minor concern.

title: From Philosophy to Practice: Defending Your Analysis
text:
8 🚨 What Did You Notice?
. . .
Let’s run Dr. Reyes’s design through our tools:
. . .
The Triangle:

Research question: Does nitrogen addition increase biomass?
Data: 12 plots nested within 4 ranches, repeated measures over time
Method: One-way ANOVA on September data only

. . .
Box Test: Is this model wrong in a way that matters?
. . .

Plots nested within ranches. Repeated measures (3 time points). One-way ANOVA ignores both.
Yes. Wrong in a way that matters.

title: From Philosophy to Practice: Defending Your Analysis
text:
9 🚨 Three Problems
. . .






Problem 1 — Habit, not design



“Standard method” is not a philosophical defense.
The design calls for lmer() with ranch as a random effect.


. . .






Problem 2 — The Triangle is broken



Repeated measures + nested structure + continuous response
→ requires a mixed model, not a one-way ANOVA.


. . .






Problem 3 — Dropping data without justification



Why only September? What does the May–July trajectory tell us?
Selective analysis ≠ rigorous inference.


. . .






Important



The fix? lmer(biomass ~ treatment * time + (1|ranch/plot), data = reyes_data)

title: From Philosophy to Practice: Defending Your Analysis
section: 10 🧭 What Kind of Inference Was Dr. Reyes Making?
text:
10 🧭 What Kind of Inference Was Dr. Reyes Making?
. . .








Type
What you need
What Dr. Reyes had




Descriptive
Representative sample
✅ (sort of)


Predictive
Cross-validation, held-out test
❌


Causal
Randomization + correct error structure
⚠️ Treatments assigned to ranches — confounded



. . .

Dr. Reyes wrote “significantly increased biomass” -> that’s a causal claim.
But the design doesn’t fully support it.

. . .






Tip



The honest version: “Biomass was higher in high-N plots (p = 0.03) –> Descriptive”

title: From Philosophy to Practice: Defending Your Analysis
section: 11 🔺 The Four Requirements of a Good Defense
text:
11 🔺 The Four Requirements of a Good Defense
. . .
Can you answer all four for your own analysis?
. . .

Philosophical grounding — What framework are you in? Frequentist? Bayesian? What does that require?

. . .

Data–method alignment — Does your method match your response variable type and data structure?

. . .

Simplicity vs. complexity — Is your model as simple as possible while still being honest?

. . .

Transparency — Could someone reproduce your analysis from your GitHub repo today?

. . .






Important



The best analysis is the simplest one that honestly addresses your question given your data structure.

title: From Philosophy to Practice: Defending Your Analysis
text:
12 🔁 Now It’s Your Turn
. . .
You just played reviewer.
. . .
Thursday’s workshop: You become the author.
. . .
You will fill out a Method Defense Card:
. . .



Prompt
Your Answer




My research question



My response variable type



My planned analysis



Why it fits (Triangle + framework)



One thing Dr. Reyes did I might be tempted to do



How I’m guarding against it

title: From Philosophy to Practice: Defending Your Analysis
text:
13 🟢🟡🔴 The Assumptions Traffic Light
Coming Thursday — but start thinking now.
. . .
Every model makes assumptions. Most people know this.
Fewer people have actually checked which ones they’ve met.
. . .



Assumption
Status




Independence of residuals
🟢 Checked


Normality of random effects
🟡


No spatial autocorrelation
🔴



. . .






Tip



The goal is not all green.
Red assumptions are honest limitations —> they belong in your Discussion.
The failure is not knowing which color you’re at.
